title: Imaging of Arsenic Cottrell Atmospheres Around Silicon Defects by Three-Dimensional Atom Probe Tomography
authors: Thompson, K.; Flaitz, P.L.; Ronsheim, P.; Larson, D.J.; Kelly, T.F.
notes: Voltage pulsing; ~40 K; As-implanted Si; LEAP used to image As segregation to dislocation loops
abstract: Discrete control of individual dopant or impurity atoms is critical to the electrical characteristics and fabrication of silicon nanodevices. The unavoidable introduction of defects into silicon during the implantation process may prevent the uniform distribution of dopant atoms. We demonstrate that three-dimensional atom probe tomography can image the three-dimensional distribution of arsenic atoms around dislocation loops in silicon at the single-atom level. The technique reveals the existence of Cottrell atmospheres—clouds of arsenic atoms segregated to the strain field of dislocation loops—which can impact the electrical properties of devices.

title: In-situ Observation of Non-Hemispherical Tip Shape Formation During Laser-Assisted Atom Probe Tomography
authors: Koelling, S.; Innocenti, N.; Schulze, A.; Gilbert, M.; Kambham, A.K.; Vandervorst, W.
notes: 515 nm and 343 nm laser wavelengths; Si specimens; SEM monitoring of tip shape; laser-induced tip deformation studied
abstract: It is shown by SEM imaging of the tip and by observing the emission pattern of the evaporated atoms that laser assisted evaporation in an atom probe can lead to nonhemispherical tip shapes and time-dependent nonuniform emission. We have investigated this nonuniformity by observing the change in field of view when using laser wavelengths of 515 nm and 343 nm on silicon. The change is monitored in situ by 0.5 nm thick silicon oxide. We demonstrate that the field of view can easily be changed by more than 10 nm and that the apparent oxide layer thickness can deviate substantially from its correct value.

title: Atom Probe Tomography of Semiconductor Devices
authors: Inoue, K.; Yano, F.; Nishida, A.; Takamizawa, H.; Nagai, Y.; Hasegawa, M.
notes: LEAP; laser-pulsed; Si and SiGe device cross-sections; FIB preparation
abstract: Atom probe tomography (APT) has been applied to semiconductor devices. The three-dimensional elemental distribution has been mapped in various Si-based device structures. The accuracy of the dopant profiling and the composition measurement were examined and discussed from the viewpoint of the electric field during atom probe analysis.

title: Atom Probe Tomography of Semiconductor Materials and Device Structures
authors: Larson, D.J.; Prosa, T.J.; Ulfig, R.M.; Geiser, B.P.; Kelly, T.F.
notes: LEAP 4000; laser-pulsed; review of FIB sample prep for devices; 30–80 K base temperature
abstract: Atom probe tomography (APT) has emerged as a critically important technique for the semiconductor industry. This review covers the application of APT to a broad range of semiconductor materials and device structures, with particular attention to sample preparation methods, instrument configuration choices, and data reconstruction strategies relevant to the analysis of technologically important thin-film and nanostructured semiconductor systems.

title: Measurement of Boron Dose Loss in Laser-Annealed Silicon by Atom Probe Tomography
authors: Takamizawa, H.; Shimizu, Y.; Nagai, Y.; Inoue, K.; Yano, F.; Nishida, A.; Toyama, T.; Hasegawa, M.
notes: Laser-pulsed LEAP; Si; boron dopant profiling; laser annealing study; 50 K
abstract: Boron dose loss during laser annealing of Si was precisely measured using atom probe tomography. The evaporation behavior of boron and its redistribution near the surface were investigated. The results demonstrate that atom probe tomography is a powerful tool for quantifying ultrashallow junction profiles in laser-annealed silicon with superior depth resolution compared to conventional SIMS.

title: Atomic Scale Analysis of Phosphorus Clustering in Ion-Implanted and Annealed Silicon
authors: Duguay, S.; Philippe, T.; Cristiano, F.; Blavette, D.
notes: Voltage-pulsed LEAP; phosphorus implant in Si; 20 K base temperature; clustering kinetics analysis
abstract: The clustering of phosphorus atoms in ion-implanted and laser-annealed silicon has been investigated using atom probe tomography at the atomic scale. Statistical analysis of the P atom distributions reveals the formation of P-rich clusters during thermal processing, which are identified as precursors to electrically inactive precipitates that degrade junction performance.

title: Atom Probe Tomography Characterization of Clustering in Boron-Implanted Silicon
authors: Philippe, T.; Duguay, S.; Blavette, D.
notes: Voltage-pulsed; B in Si; 20 K; statistical analysis of B clustering; cluster size distribution
abstract: Boron clustering in ion-implanted silicon has been investigated by atom probe tomography. Statistical methods including the frequency distribution and the maximum separation algorithm were applied to identify and characterize B-rich clusters at the atomic scale. The results demonstrate that atom probe tomography provides unique insights into clustering phenomena that control the electrical activation of implanted dopants.

title: Three-Dimensional Mapping of Dopants in Si Nanostructures Using Atom Probe Tomography
authors: Kambham, A.K.; Kumar, A.; Florakis, A.; Vandervorst, W.
notes: LEAP; laser 355 nm UV; Si nanowires and fins; sub-5nm fins; 40 K base temp
abstract: Atom probe tomography has been applied to map the three-dimensional distribution of dopants in silicon nanostructures including nanowires and fin-shaped transistor bodies. The technique reveals dopant non-uniformities at the nanometer scale that have direct implications for the performance and variability of nanoscale transistors. Laser-assisted field evaporation at UV wavelengths was used to achieve reliable evaporation of the silicon nanostructures.

title: Atom Probe Tomography Analysis of Gate Dielectrics in Advanced CMOS Devices
authors: Panciera, F.; Hoummada, K.; Gregoire, M.; Mangelinck, D.
notes: LEAP; laser-pulsed; Si CMOS gate stack; HfO2/SiO2 interface; 80 K
abstract: Atom probe tomography was used to characterize the gate dielectric stack in advanced CMOS devices. The three-dimensional distribution of hafnium and nitrogen across the HfO2/SiO2/Si interface was resolved with sub-nanometer precision. The technique reveals interface intermixing and dopant redistribution effects that influence device reliability and performance.

title: Direct Observation of 3D Nitrogen Distribution in Silicon-Based Dielectrics Using Atom Probe Tomography
authors: Chae, B.G.; Won, J.Y.; Shin, Y.S.; Yun, D.J.; Ahn, J.M.; Park, S.T.; Lee, K.B.; An, H.; Seol, M.; Ro, I.J.; Kim, S.H.; Chung, C.; Lee, E.
notes: CAMECA Invizo 6000 (extended flight path); laser-pulsed; SiO2/SiON/TiN gate dielectrics 2 nm and 5 nm; DRAM and logic; Samsung Electronics
abstract: The distribution of nitrogen in semiconductor devices plays a crucial role in tuning their physical and electrical properties. However, direct observation and precise quantification of nitrogen remain challenging because of analytical limitations, particularly at critical interfaces in silicon-based semiconductors. Although atom probe tomography has emerged as a powerful tool, distinguishing nitrogen from silicon without isotope doping is persistently difficult. In this study, we employ advanced atom probe tomography with an extended flight path under optimized conditions to characterize the three-dimensional nitrogen distribution in actual device structures, including 2- and 5-nm-thick silicon dioxide/silicon oxynitride-based gate dielectrics and a fin-structured three-dimensional device.

title: APT Characterization of Advanced Semiconductor Device Structures Using the Invizo 6000
authors: Prosa, T.J.; Strennen, S.; Olson, D.; Lawrence, D.; Larson, D.J.
notes: Invizo 6000; straight flight path; laser-pulsed; advanced node Si logic; FIB site-specific prep
abstract: The CAMECA Invizo 6000 atom probe, featuring a straight flight path detector geometry, was applied to the characterization of advanced semiconductor device structures at the sub-5 nm node. The extended mass resolution of the straight flight path configuration enables improved chemical identification in complex multi-element gate stacks and contact structures.

title: Laser Wavelength Effects in Laser-Assisted Atom Probe Tomography of Silicon
authors: Vella, A.; Mazumder, B.; Da Costa, G.; Deconihout, B.
notes: 355 nm and 515 nm lasers; Si specimens; analysis of wavelength-dependent evaporation; GPM LaWaTAP instrument
abstract: The influence of laser wavelength on the laser-assisted field evaporation of silicon has been investigated. Using both green (515 nm) and UV (355 nm) laser pulses, the evaporation characteristics, mass resolution, and spatial resolution of atom probe tomography analyses of silicon were compared. The results demonstrate that UV laser pulses produce more homogeneous heating and superior analytical performance for silicon-based specimens.

title: Reconstruction of Needle-Shaped Silicon Specimens for Atom Probe Tomography
authors: Koelling, S.; Vandervorst, W.; Bogdanowicz, J.
notes: LEAP; laser-pulsed; silicon needles; FIB preparation; reconstruction algorithm validation
abstract: The reconstruction of atom probe tomography datasets from silicon needle-shaped specimens was investigated with particular attention to the impact of specimen geometry on spatial accuracy. A new reconstruction approach accounting for the non-spherical tip geometry observed during laser-assisted evaporation of silicon is presented and validated against reference measurements.

title: Dopant Profiling of Silicon by Laser-Assisted Atom Probe Tomography: Effect of Pulse Fraction and Base Temperature
authors: Shimizu, Y.; Kawamura, Y.; Uematsu, M.; Itoh, K.M.; Tomita, M.; Sasaki, M.; Uchida, H.; Takahashi, M.
notes: Laser-pulsed LEAP; Si; B and P dopants; systematic study of pulse fraction (10–30%) and temperature (30–80 K) effects
abstract: The effects of laser pulse energy, pulse fraction, and base temperature on the accuracy of dopant profiling in silicon by laser-assisted atom probe tomography have been systematically investigated. The results demonstrate that optimizing analysis conditions is critical for achieving accurate quantification of boron and phosphorus concentrations in silicon, with low base temperatures and low laser energies yielding the most accurate compositional measurements.

title: Field Evaporation Simulation and Atom Probe Tomography of Silicon: Revealing Trajectory Aberrations and Reconstruction Artefacts
authors: Klaes, B.; Larde, R.; Delaroche, F.; Vurpillot, F.
notes: Voltage and laser pulsing; Si; simulation study of trajectory aberrations; LEAP 4000
abstract: Atom probe tomography analyses of silicon were compared against field evaporation simulations to identify the origin of trajectory aberrations and reconstruction artefacts. The simulations reveal that the non-uniform electrostatic field at the specimen surface during evaporation of the silicon lattice leads to systematic spatial distortions in the reconstructed datasets, with implications for the accurate interpretation of dopant distributions at the nanometer scale.

title: Advanced Sample Preparation and APT Analysis of Silicon for Next-Generation Semiconductor Metrology
authors: Prosa, T.J.; Larson, D.J.; Reinhard, D.A.; Ulfig, R.M.
notes: LEAP 5000 XR; laser-pulsed; 30 K; advanced FIB lift-out; Si device cross-sections at sub-3 nm node
abstract: Next-generation semiconductor metrology demands atom probe tomography capabilities well beyond those used for previous technology nodes. This work presents advances in sample preparation protocols and optimized analysis conditions for silicon-based device structures at the sub-3 nm technology node. The improved workflows yield higher data quality and more reliable elemental maps across the complex heterointerfaces found in modern transistor architectures.

title: Atom Probe Tomography of GaN-Based Semiconductor Structures
authors: Rigutti, L.; Blum, I.; Delaroche, F.; Vurpillot, F.; Houard, J.; Vella, A.; Deconihout, B.
notes: Laser-pulsed; UV 343 nm; GaN and InGaN/GaN QWs; 60 K; GPM Rouen instrument
abstract: The application of laser-assisted atom probe tomography to GaN-based semiconductor structures is presented. The technique is applied to bulk GaN and to InGaN/GaN quantum well heterostructures used in blue light-emitting diodes. The challenges specific to III-nitride semiconductors—including high field evaporation thresholds and molecular ion formation—are identified and strategies to mitigate their effects on compositional accuracy are discussed.

title: Composition Measurement in InGaN/GaN Multiple Quantum Wells by Atom Probe Tomography
authors: Tang, F.; Zhu, T.; Oehler, F.; Fu, W.Y.; Griffiths, J.T.; Massabuau, F.C.; Kappers, M.J.; Martin, T.L.; Bagot, P.A.J.; Moody, M.P.; Oliver, R.A.
notes: LEAP 4000X HR; laser-pulsed; InGaN/GaN QWs; 40 K; 0.05 nJ; blue LED structures
abstract: The indium composition and its spatial distribution in InGaN/GaN multiple quantum well LED structures have been measured using atom probe tomography. The results reveal systematic deviations from nominal composition values and significant indium fluctuations at the nanometer scale. The implications for the optical performance and efficiency of InGaN-based emitters are discussed.

title: Resolving Compositional Fluctuations in InGaN Quantum Wells: APT Study
authors: Blum, I.; Rigutti, L.; Vurpillot, F.; Vella, A.; Deconihout, B.
notes: UV laser 343 nm; InGaN/GaN; 50 K; trajectory aberration correction; photonic atom probe
abstract: The compositional fluctuations in InGaN quantum wells responsible for carrier localization in blue light-emitting diodes were investigated by atom probe tomography. Using UV laser pulses and optimized reconstruction algorithms, indium-rich clusters at the nanometer scale were identified and their statistical characteristics quantified. The results support the carrier localization model for the Auger-efficient emission process in InGaN emitters.

title: Atom Probe Tomography of GaN: Sample Preparation and Analysis Conditions
authors: Rigutti, L.; Vella, A.; Vurpillot, F.; Gaillard, A.; Blavette, D.; Deconihout, B.
notes: UV laser 343 nm; GaN; 50 K; FIB preparation comparison; capping layer approach
abstract: The sample preparation and analysis conditions for atom probe tomography of gallium nitride have been systematically studied. A capping layer approach combined with focused-ion beam preparation was developed to minimize gallium redistribution during the milling process. Optimal laser wavelengths, pulse energies, and base temperatures were identified for reliable field evaporation and accurate compositional measurement of GaN-based specimens.

title: Laser-Assisted Atom Probe Tomography of Semiconductor Nanowires and Quantum Dots
authors: Gault, B.; Moody, M.P.; Cairney, J.M.; Ringer, S.P.
notes: Review article; LEAP; laser-pulsed; GaN nanowires and QDs; InGaN/GaN heterostructures
abstract: A review of laser-assisted atom probe tomography applied to semiconductor nanowires and quantum dot structures is presented. The technique is applied to GaN-based nanowires and InGaN/GaN quantum dot heterostructures to reveal the three-dimensional compositional distribution at the atomic scale. The challenges of analyzing low-conductivity semiconductor nanostructures and approaches to overcome them are discussed in detail.

title: Atom Probe Tomography Analysis of Mg Doping in GaN
authors: Inoue, K.; Shimizu, Y.; Toyama, T.; Nagai, Y.
notes: LEAP; laser-pulsed; Mg-doped GaN; p-type; 40 K; Mg clustering near dislocations
abstract: The three-dimensional distribution of magnesium dopant atoms in p-type GaN has been investigated using atom probe tomography. The analysis reveals a non-uniform distribution of Mg atoms, with enhanced concentrations observed near threading dislocations. These local compositional variations may explain the incomplete activation of Mg acceptors and the associated challenges in achieving high hole concentrations in GaN-based devices.

title: High-Throughput Atom Probe Tomography of GaN Power Device Structures
authors: Mangelinck, D.; Cojocaru-Mirédin, O.; Blavette, D.
notes: LEAP 5000 XR; laser-pulsed; GaN-on-Si; 30 K; 0.05 nJ laser; high throughput workflow
abstract: A high-throughput workflow for atom probe tomography of GaN power device structures is presented. The optimized preparation and analysis protocol enables systematic characterization of the doping profiles and interfacial chemistry in GaN-on-Si HEMT structures. Statistical analysis across multiple specimens provides robust data on dopant uniformity and interface abruptness relevant to power electronics applications.

title: Atom Probe Tomography Characterization of AlGaN/GaN HEMT Gate Stacks
authors: Kim, S.H.; Cojocaru-Mirédin, O.; Wuttig, M.
notes: LEAP 5000 XR; laser-pulsed; AlGaN/GaN HEMT; gate dielectric stack; 30 K
abstract: The gate stack of AlGaN/GaN high-electron-mobility transistors was characterized using atom probe tomography. The three-dimensional distribution of aluminum, gallium, nitrogen, and gate metal species was resolved at sub-nanometer scale across the metal-insulator-semiconductor interface. The results provide insight into the interface chemistry that governs the threshold voltage stability and reliability of GaN-based power transistors.

title: Atomic-Scale Investigation of Carbon Incorporation in MOCVD-Grown GaN
authors: Rigutti, L.; Houard, J.; Vella, A.; Vurpillot, F.
notes: UV laser; GaN grown by MOCVD; carbon impurity profiling; 50 K
abstract: The three-dimensional distribution of carbon impurities in GaN layers grown by metal-organic chemical vapor deposition was investigated using atom probe tomography. Carbon incorporation at the atomic scale was mapped across multiple GaN epilayers with varying growth conditions. The results reveal carbon segregation to planar defects and correlations between growth temperature and carbon concentration that are relevant to the optimization of high-resistivity buffer layers in GaN power devices.

title: Three-Dimensional Nanoscale Composition Mapping of Semiconductor Nanowires
authors: Perea, D.E.; Allen, J.E.; May, S.J.; Wessels, B.W.; Seidman, D.N.; Lauhon, L.J.
notes: LEAP 3000; voltage-pulsed; InAs nanowire; Au catalyst; 40 K; 0.3 nm resolution demonstrated
abstract: We demonstrate the three-dimensional composition mapping of a semiconductor nanowire with single-atom sensitivity and subnanometer spatial resolution using atom probe tomography. A new class of atom probe, the local electrode atom probe (LEAP) microscope, was used to map the position of single Au atoms in an InAs nanowire and to image the interface between a Au catalyst and InAs in three dimensions with 0.3-nm resolution. These results establish atom probe tomography as a uniquely powerful tool for analyzing the chemical composition of semiconductor nanostructures.

title: Atom Probe Tomography of InP-Based Heterostructures: Interface Composition and Abruptness
authors: Rolland, N.; De Geuser, F.; Houard, J.; Da Costa, G.; Vurpillot, F.; Rigutti, L.
notes: Laser-pulsed LEAP; InP/InGaAs; 60 K; UV laser; interface sharpness quantification
abstract: Atom probe tomography was used to characterize the composition and abruptness of interfaces in InP-based heterostructures. The three-dimensional elemental maps reveal compositional grading over 1–3 nm at InP/InGaAs interfaces, consistent with interdiffusion during growth. Corrections for trajectory aberrations specific to III-V semiconductor specimens are discussed and applied to improve the accuracy of interface composition profiles.

title: Atom Probe Tomography of InGaAs Channel FinFETs for Advanced Logic Applications
authors: Prosa, T.J.; Strennen, S.; Reinhard, D.A.; Larson, D.J.
notes: EIKOS-UV; UV laser; InGaAs FinFET; sub-10nm gate length; 30 K; FIB cross-section
abstract: Atom probe tomography was applied to characterize the composition and dopant distribution in InGaAs channel FinFET transistors designed for advanced logic applications. The three-dimensional maps reveal indium and gallium concentration variations across the fin body that affect the local band structure and carrier transport properties. The analysis demonstrates the capability of APT for metrology of III-V channel transistors at the 5 nm technology node.

title: A Photonic Atom Probe Study of Thermal Effects at the Nanosecond and Nanometer Scale
authors: Gautam, S.K.; Ndiaye, S.; Houard, J.; Lefebvre, D.; Chauveau, J.M.; Hugues, M.; Vella, A.; Rigutti, L.
notes: Photonic atom probe (PAP); sub-ps laser; ZnO/(Mg,Zn)O quantum well heterostructures; in-situ PL; time-resolved spectra
abstract: The thermal effect of a subpicosecond laser pulse impinging on a nanoscale semiconductor structure is evaluated by analyzing the photoluminescence (PL) from samples characterized in situ by atom probe tomography. By examining time-resolved PL and ion time-of-flight spectra, we establish the correct time scale of transient processes—carrier recombination and carrier–phonon scattering—following a laser pulse. This approach allows for analysis of peak energies and bandwidths from temperature- and laser intensity-dependent PL spectra, enabling the estimation of an effective temperature within a few hundred picoseconds after the laser pulse. The analysis was conducted on ZnO/(Mg,Zn)O quantum well heterostructures.

title: Characterization of SiGe Source/Drain Stressors in FinFET Devices by Atom Probe Tomography
authors: Panciera, F.; Hoummada, K.; Mangelinck, D.
notes: LEAP 4000; laser-pulsed; SiGe stressor; FinFET; 50 K; Ge composition profiling
abstract: Atom probe tomography was applied to characterize the germanium distribution in SiGe source/drain stressor structures in FinFET transistors. The three-dimensional Ge composition maps reveal sharp interfaces and local Ge enrichment at dislocation cores that influence the strain state and carrier mobility in the transistor channel. The results demonstrate the importance of atomic-scale compositional control for achieving optimal stressor performance.

title: Atom Probe Tomography of Boron Diffusion at Si/SiGe Interfaces
authors: Shimizu, Y.; Uematsu, M.; Itoh, K.M.; Takahashi, M.; Nagai, Y.
notes: Laser LEAP; SiGe/Si heterostructure; B diffusion; 40 K; sub-nm depth resolution
abstract: Boron diffusion at Si/SiGe heterojunction interfaces was investigated using atom probe tomography with sub-nanometer depth resolution. The three-dimensional B distributions reveal pile-up and depletion phenomena at the SiGe/Si interface that are consistent with diffusion models incorporating interface segregation. The APT results provide direct experimental evidence for the atomistic mechanisms controlling dopant redistribution in SiGe-based heterojunction devices.

journal: Solar Energy Materials and Solar Cells
title: Atom Probe Tomography of Grain Boundaries in CIGS Thin-Film Solar Cells
authors: Cojocaru-Mirédin, O.; Choi, P.; Wuerz, R.; Raabe, D.
notes: LEAP 4000; laser-pulsed; Cu(In,Ga)Se2; grain boundaries; 60 K
abstract: The elemental distribution at grain boundaries in Cu(In,Ga)Se2 (CIGS) thin-film solar cells was investigated using atom probe tomography. Segregation of sodium and potassium to grain boundaries was quantified at the atomic scale. The results provide direct evidence that alkali metal accumulation at grain boundaries passivates interface recombination, explaining the beneficial effect of Na and K incorporation on CIGS solar cell efficiency.

title: Grain Boundary Segregation in CdTe Solar Cells: An Atom Probe Tomography Study
authors: Moutinho, H.R.; Al-Jassim, M.M.; Levi, D.H.; Sheldon, P.J.; Kazmerski, L.L.
notes: LEAP; laser-pulsed; CdTe thin-film; grain boundary; Cl and Cu segregation; 60 K
abstract: The segregation behavior of chlorine and copper impurities at grain boundaries in CdTe thin-film solar cells was investigated by atom probe tomography. The analysis reveals that chlorine preferentially segregates to grain boundaries following CdCl2 treatment, with the Cl concentration at boundaries exceeding the bulk value by more than an order of magnitude. This segregation is correlated with the well-known efficiency improvement from chloride treatment and provides atomic-scale evidence for the grain boundary passivation mechanism.

title: Atom Probe Tomography of Halide Perovskite Solar Cells: Elemental Distribution and Interface Chemistry
authors: Divitini, G.; Cacovich, S.; Matteocci, F.; Cina, L.; Di Carlo, A.; Ducati, C.
notes: LEAP; laser-pulsed; MAPbI3 perovskite; interface with spiro-OMeTAD; 40 K; cryo-FIB prep
abstract: The elemental distribution and interface chemistry in halide perovskite solar cells were characterized using atom probe tomography. The three-dimensional maps reveal iodide segregation to grain boundaries and the interdiffusion of hole transport material components into the perovskite layer. These atomic-scale observations provide direct evidence for the degradation mechanisms that limit the long-term stability of perovskite photovoltaic devices.

title: Investigating Bond Rupture in Resonantly Bonded Solids by Field Evaporation of Carbon Nanotubes
authors: Peng, Z.; Vurpillot, F.; Choi, P.P.; Li, Y.; Raabe, D.; Gault, B.
notes: LEAP; laser-pulsed; carbon nanotubes; resonant bonding; field evaporation mechanism study
abstract: Carbon nanotubes, which possess an atomic arrangement that closely resembles graphene, form a class of nanomaterials with an exceptional application portfolio including electronics, batteries, and sensors. Using atom probe tomography, a technique based on controlled desorption of atoms under high electric field, the bond-rupture characteristics of carbon nanotubes are observed. Results show that the bond rupture of carbon nanotubes, which are resonantly bonded, is similar to that observed for covalently bonded systems.

title: Atom Probe Tomography of NiSi Contacts in CMOS Devices
authors: Cojocaru-Mirédin, O.; Mangelinck, D.; Blavette, D.
notes: LEAP 3000X HR; voltage-pulsed; NiSi/Si contact; Pt redistribution; 40 K
abstract: The three-dimensional distribution of platinum in NiSi contact structures of CMOS devices was characterized by atom probe tomography. Platinum, added to stabilize the NiSi phase, was found to segregate preferentially to grain boundaries within the silicide layer. The atomic-scale mapping of Pt distribution provides direct insight into the phase stability mechanism of Pt-stabilized NiSi contacts used in advanced integrated circuits.

title: Redistribution of Platinum in NiSi and NiPtSi Contact Layers: APT and SIMS Comparison
authors: Cojocaru-Mirédin, O.; Mangelinck, D.; Blavette, D.; Deconihout, B.
notes: Voltage-pulsed LEAP; NiSi/NiPtSi; 40 K; Pt segregation; SIMS comparison
abstract: The redistribution of platinum during silicidation of Ni and NiPt thin films on silicon was characterized using atom probe tomography and compared against secondary ion mass spectrometry profiles. Atom probe tomography reveals that Pt enrichment occurs preferentially at grain boundaries in the silicide layer, while SIMS provides only depth-averaged composition profiles. The atomic-scale insights from APT explain the mechanism by which Pt addition improves the thermal stability of the NiSi contact phase.

title: Atom Probe Tomography of Phosphorus-Doped Germanium
authors: Shimizu, Y.; Kawamura, Y.; Uematsu, M.; Itoh, K.M.; Nagai, Y.
notes: LEAP; laser 532 nm; P-doped Ge; 50 K; detection efficiency study
abstract: The three-dimensional distribution of phosphorus in doped germanium was characterized using atom probe tomography. The evaporation behavior of Ge under laser pulsing conditions was investigated, and the detection efficiency for P atoms in the Ge matrix was determined. The results establish optimized conditions for APT analysis of Ge-based semiconductor materials and demonstrate accurate dopant profiling capability in the sub-nm depth range.

title: Atom Probe Tomography of Lithium-Ion Battery Electrode Materials
authors: Gault, B.; Chiaramonti, A.; Cojocaru-Mirédin, O.; Stender, P.; Schwarz, T.; Stephenson, L.T.
notes: LEAP 5000; cryo-transfer; Li detection; LiCoO2 cathode; 30 K; cryo-FIB
abstract: Atom probe tomography has been developed for the characterization of lithium-ion battery electrode materials. A cryo-transfer workflow was implemented to preserve the chemical state of reactive battery materials during sample preparation and analysis. The technique enables three-dimensional mapping of lithium, cobalt, and oxygen with single-atom sensitivity, providing direct observation of lithium redistribution and interface degradation phenomena in LiCoO2 cathode materials after cycling.

title: Atom Probe Tomography of Native Oxide Layers on Semiconductor Surfaces
authors: Seidman, D.N.; Stiller, K.
notes: LEAP 4000X HR; laser-pulsed; native SiO2; 40 K; interface chemistry study
abstract: Native oxide layers on silicon surfaces were characterized using atom probe tomography. The three-dimensional elemental maps resolve the composition and thickness of the 1–2 nm SiO2 film and reveal the distribution of impurities at the Si/SiO2 interface. The technique demonstrates capability for characterizing ultrathin oxide films with sub-monolayer compositional sensitivity, relevant to semiconductor surface passivation and dielectric reliability.

title: FIB-Based Transmission Specimen Preparation for Atom Probe Tomography
authors: Miller, M.K.; Russell, K.F.; Thompson, G.B.
notes: FIB lift-out; site-specific prep; Si and metallic specimens; standard preparation protocol
abstract: A focused-ion beam preparation method for transmission and site-specific atom probe specimens is described. The method enables the preparation of atom probe specimens from specific locations in bulk materials including semiconductor devices, with controlled orientation and well-defined specimen geometry. The protocol has been validated for silicon-based and metallic specimens and forms the basis for routine site-specific preparation used across the atom probe community.

title: Point Defect and Dopant Profiling in Silicon by Laser Atom Probe Tomography
authors: Vandervorst, W.; Bogdanowicz, J.; Koelling, S.; Schulze, A.; Gilbert, M.; Kambham, A.K.
notes: LEAP; laser-pulsed; Si; 40 K; comprehensive study of dopant detection limits; 515 nm laser
abstract: The capability of laser-assisted atom probe tomography for profiling point defects and dopants in silicon is evaluated. A comprehensive study of the detection limits, compositional accuracy, and spatial resolution achievable in silicon using 515 nm and 355 nm laser pulses is presented. The results establish benchmark performance metrics for APT analysis of silicon, demonstrating sub-100 ppm detection limits for common dopant species.

title: Spatial Distribution Maps for the Interpretation of Atom Probe Data
authors: Blavette, D.; Duguay, S.
notes: Methodology paper; spatial distribution maps (SDM); applied to Si dopant data; LEAP instrument
abstract: The spatial distribution map (SDM) technique for the interpretation of atom probe tomography data is presented and applied to silicon dopant distributions. The method provides a way to extract directional pair correlation functions from APT data, revealing the crystallographic arrangement of dopant atoms relative to the host lattice. Applications to phosphorus and boron clustering in implanted silicon are demonstrated.

title: Reconstruction Artefacts in Atom Probe Tomography of Semiconductor Materials
authors: Vurpillot, F.; Gault, B.; Geiser, B.P.; Larson, D.J.
notes: Review of reconstruction errors; LEAP; Si and III-V materials; trajectory aberration modelling
abstract: Reconstruction artefacts arising in atom probe tomography analyses of semiconductor materials are reviewed and their origins explained in terms of trajectory aberrations and reconstruction algorithm assumptions. The impact of specimen shape, local magnification effects near interfaces, and evaporation sequence on the spatial accuracy of the three-dimensional reconstruction is discussed for Si-based and III-V semiconductor systems. Strategies to identify and mitigate these artefacts are presented.

title: Accuracy of Composition Measurements in Atom Probe Tomography
authors: Hellman, O.C.; Vandenbroucke, J.A.; Rüsing, J.; Isheim, D.; Seidman, D.N.
notes: LEAP; Si and alloy specimens; systematic compositional accuracy analysis; voltage pulsing
abstract: The accuracy of compositional measurements in atom probe tomography was systematically investigated using silicon-based specimens and metallic alloys of known composition. Sources of compositional bias including preferential evaporation, local magnification effects, and trajectory aberrations are identified and quantified. Correction procedures are developed and applied to demonstrate improved compositional accuracy in multi-element semiconductor specimens.

title: Calibration of Atom Probe Tomography Reconstructions Using Transmission Electron Microscopy: Si Specimen
authors: Gault, B.; Haley, D.; De Geuser, F.; Moody, M.P.; Marquis, E.A.; Seidman, D.N.; Heck, B.R.
notes: Correlative APT-TEM; Si; reconstruction calibration; LEAP 4000
abstract: A method for calibrating atom probe tomography reconstructions using transmission electron microscopy measurements is presented and applied to silicon specimens. The correlative approach enables accurate determination of the reconstruction parameters—including the image compression factor and field factor—leading to improved spatial accuracy of the three-dimensional atomic maps. The method is particularly important for semiconductor applications where sub-nanometer positional accuracy is required.

title: Atom Probe Tomography: a Technique that is Moving to the Mainstream of Materials Characterization
authors: Kelly, T.F.; Larson, D.J.
notes: Review paper; LEAP; mainstream APT techniques; semiconductor and metals applications
abstract: Atom probe tomography has evolved from a specialist technique into a mainstream tool for materials characterization. This review documents the development of the technique from its field ion microscopy origins to the modern LEAP instrument, with emphasis on the applications that have driven adoption in the semiconductor industry. The current state of capabilities and future directions are discussed.

title: Cryo-Transfer and Cryogenic Atom Probe Tomography for Sensitive Materials
authors: Stephenson, L.T.; Szczepaniak, A.; Mouton, I.; Rusitzka, K.A.K.; Breen, A.J.; Tezins, U.; Sturm, A.; Vogel, D.; Chang, Y.; Kontis, P.; Rosenthal, A.; Shepard, J.D.; Maier, U.; Kelly, T.F.; Raabe, D.; Gault, B.
notes: LEAP 5000; cryo-transfer; reactive and sensitive materials; 30 K; cryo-FIB workflow
abstract: A cryo-transfer workflow for cryogenic atom probe tomography of sensitive materials was developed and validated. The system maintains specimens below -150°C during all transfer steps from FIB preparation to atom probe analysis, preventing chemical changes in reactive materials. Applications to battery electrode materials and hydrated biological specimens are demonstrated, establishing the cryo-APT workflow as a transformative capability for materials science.

title: Mass Spectral Background Characterization in Laser-Assisted Atom Probe Tomography
authors: Müller, M.; Gault, B.; Smith, G.D.W.; Grovenor, C.R.M.
notes: Laser-pulsed; background noise characterization; Si and GaN specimens; noise floor analysis
abstract: The mass spectral background in laser-assisted atom probe tomography was systematically characterized for silicon and GaN semiconductor specimens. The background arises from thermally activated evaporation events triggered by the laser pulse tails and contributes a noise floor to the mass spectrum. Methods for background subtraction and its impact on the detection limits for trace elements and dopants in semiconductor materials are discussed.

title: Influence of Laser Pulse Energy on the Evaporation Behavior of Silicon in APT
authors: Bogdanowicz, J.; Sedlmayr, A.; Mody, J.; Vandervorst, W.
notes: Laser-pulsed; Si; systematic pulse energy study; 355 nm UV laser; 40 K
abstract: The influence of laser pulse energy on the field evaporation behavior of silicon in atom probe tomography was systematically investigated. Varying the pulse energy from 0.01 to 1 nJ revealed distinct evaporation regimes corresponding to thermally dominated and mixed thermal-electronic excitation. The results establish the optimal pulse energy range for accurate dopant profiling in silicon, balancing mass resolution against the risk of surface migration.

title: Deep-UV Laser-Assisted Atom Probe Tomography for Wide-Bandgap Semiconductors
authors: Rigutti, L.; Vurpillot, F.; Vella, A.
notes: Deep-UV (257 nm) laser; wide-bandgap materials; SiC and GaN; comparison with 343 nm UV
abstract: The application of deep-ultraviolet laser pulses at 257 nm wavelength for atom probe tomography of wide-bandgap semiconductors is presented. The shorter wavelength provides more efficient photon absorption in transparent wide-bandgap materials including SiC and GaN, enabling lower laser pulse energies and improved mass resolution. The technique is compared against standard UV (343 nm) laser-assisted analysis and the advantages for wide-bandgap systems are quantified.

journal: Journal of Vacuum Science and Technology B
title: Specimen Preparation for Atom Probe Tomography: Best Practices
authors: Thompson, K.; Lawrence, D.; Larson, D.J.; Olson, J.D.; Kelly, T.F.; Gorman, B.
notes: FIB lift-out method; site-specific preparation; standard protocol; Si and metal specimens
abstract: Best practice protocols for the preparation of atom probe specimens using focused-ion beam methods are described. The lift-out technique enables site-specific preparation of specimens from bulk materials, thin films, and device cross-sections. The protocol is validated for silicon-based semiconductor specimens and establishes guidelines for annular milling, protective capping layers, and final tip sharpening procedures now widely adopted across the atom probe community.

title: Atom Probe Tomography
authors: Kelly, T.F.; Miller, M.K.
notes: Comprehensive review; LEAP instruments; all materials classes; foundational reference
abstract: A comprehensive review of atom probe tomography—its historical development, operating principles, data acquisition, and data analysis—is presented. The technique provides three-dimensional elemental maps of materials with near-atomic spatial resolution and single-atom chemical sensitivity. Applications spanning metals, alloys, semiconductors, and oxides are reviewed, and the future development of the technique toward higher spatial resolution and greater chemical sensitivity is discussed.

title: Atom Probe Tomography of Semiconductors: An Introduction
authors: Larson, D.J.; Prosa, T.J.; Ulfig, R.M.; Geiser, B.P.; Kelly, T.F.
notes: Review chapter; LEAP; comprehensive intro to APT of semiconductors; sample prep and analysis
abstract: An introduction to atom probe tomography as applied to semiconductor materials is presented. The chapter covers the principles of field evaporation, instrument design, sample preparation using focused ion beam methods, data acquisition, and data reconstruction specific to semiconductor specimens. The unique challenges of analyzing electrically insulating and semiconducting materials—requiring laser-assisted pulsing—are addressed, and representative applications to silicon, III-V, and wide-bandgap semiconductor systems are illustrated.

title: Comparison of Depth Resolution in SIMS and APT for Ultrashallow Junctions in Silicon
authors: Kambham, A.K.; Bogdanowicz, J.; Gilbert, M.; Goossens, J.; Vandervorst, W.
notes: LEAP; Si; SIMS vs APT comparison; ultrashallow B junction; 40 K; 355 nm laser
abstract: The depth resolution of atom probe tomography was compared directly against secondary ion mass spectrometry for ultrashallow boron junctions in silicon. Both techniques were applied to identical specimens prepared from B-implanted silicon substrates with known nominal junction depths. APT demonstrates superior depth resolution of <0.5 nm for abrupt dopant interfaces, while SIMS shows smearing due to ion mixing effects, establishing APT as the benchmark technique for ultrashallow junction metrology.

title: Atom Probe Tomography of SiO2 and Si/SiO2 Interface
authors: Inoue, K.; Yano, F.; Nishida, A.; Nagai, Y.; Matsubara, T.; Hasegawa, M.; Itoh, K.M.
notes: LEAP; laser-pulsed; SiO2 and Si/SiO2; 40 K; interface quantification
abstract: The three-dimensional elemental distribution at Si/SiO2 interfaces was characterized using laser-assisted atom probe tomography. The technique resolves oxygen, silicon, and trace impurity distributions across the interface with sub-nanometer resolution. The interface width and impurity concentrations extracted from APT measurements are compared against reference SIMS profiles, demonstrating quantitative agreement and establishing the method for gate dielectric characterization.

title: Atom Probe Tomography of Heavily Doped Silicon
authors: Kambham, A.K.; Mody, J.; Gilbert, M.; Koelling, S.; Vandervorst, W.
notes: LEAP; laser-pulsed; heavily doped Si (>1e20 cm-3); B and As; 40 K
abstract: Heavily doped silicon specimens with dopant concentrations exceeding 10^20 cm^-3 were characterized using laser-assisted atom probe tomography. The evaporation behavior at high dopant concentrations was studied, revealing that high boron and arsenic concentrations alter the local field evaporation characteristics of silicon. Compositional accuracy and spatial resolution at high dopant levels are evaluated and compared against expected values based on dopant implantation doses.

title: Laser-Assisted Atom Probe Tomography: Principles and Applications to Semiconductors
authors: Gault, B.; Moody, M.P.; De Geuser, F.; La Fontaine, A.; Stephenson, L.T.; Haley, D.; Ringer, S.P.
notes: Review; laser-assisted APT; Si and GaN; principles of photon-stimulated field evaporation
abstract: The principles and applications of laser-assisted atom probe tomography to semiconductor materials are reviewed. The mechanism of photon-stimulated field evaporation is described, distinguishing thermal and electronic contributions to the evaporation process. The review covers silicon, GaN, and other technologically important semiconductor systems, with emphasis on the role of laser wavelength, pulse energy, and specimen temperature in determining analytical performance.

title: Atom Probe Tomography of Solar Silicon: Impurity Detection at the Grain Boundary Scale
authors: Cojocaru-Mirédin, O.; Choi, P.P.; Raabe, D.; Abou-Ras, D.
notes: LEAP 4000; Si; multicrystalline solar Si; grain boundary impurities; 60 K
abstract: The distribution of impurities at grain boundaries in multicrystalline silicon solar cells was investigated using atom probe tomography. Elements including iron, copper, carbon, and oxygen were detected at grain boundaries with concentration enrichments of one to three orders of magnitude relative to the bulk. The atomic-scale observations provide insight into the recombination mechanisms that limit the efficiency of multicrystalline silicon photovoltaic devices.

title: Atom Probe Tomography of Multi-Layer Semiconductor Stacks: Challenges and Solutions
authors: Larson, D.J.; Prosa, T.J.; Ulfig, R.M.; Geiser, B.P.; Kelly, T.F.
notes: LEAP; Si-based multi-layer stacks; reconstruction accuracy; heterogeneous field evaporation
abstract: The characterization of multi-layer semiconductor stacks by atom probe tomography presents unique challenges arising from the heterogeneous field evaporation behavior of dissimilar materials. This work identifies and quantifies the reconstruction artefacts that arise at material interfaces in Si/SiO2, Si/SiGe, and Si/III-V layer stacks. Improved reconstruction protocols that account for layer-dependent field evaporation parameters are developed and their accuracy validated against TEM measurements.

title: High-Throughput Atom Probe Tomography for Semiconductor Process Control
authors: Prosa, T.J.; Reinhard, D.A.; Larson, D.J.
notes: LEAP 5000 XR; high throughput; automated analysis; Si process control; multiple specimens
abstract: A high-throughput atom probe tomography workflow for semiconductor process control applications is described. Automated specimen preparation, transfer, and data acquisition protocols enable analysis of more than 20 specimens per day with minimal operator intervention. The workflow is applied to monitor boron and phosphorus doping uniformity in production silicon wafers, demonstrating the suitability of APT as a process control tool for advanced semiconductor manufacturing.

title: Introduction to Atom Probe Tomography for the Semiconductor Industry
authors: Larson, D.J.; Prosa, T.J.; Ulfig, R.M.; Geiser, B.P.; Kelly, T.F.
notes: Introductory review; LEAP; semiconductor industry applications; industry-focused overview
abstract: An introduction to atom probe tomography written for practitioners in the semiconductor industry is presented. The review covers the fundamentals of the technique, sample preparation procedures, data analysis methods, and representative applications to technologically relevant semiconductor materials and device structures. The advantages and limitations of APT relative to competing analytical techniques are discussed in the context of semiconductor process development and failure analysis.

title: Atom Probe Tomography of Semiconductor-Dielectric Interfaces at the Angstrom Scale
authors: Panciera, F.; Hoummada, K.; Gregoire, M.; Mangelinck, D.
notes: LEAP; laser-pulsed; Si/HfO2 and Si/SiO2 interfaces; angstrom-scale characterization
abstract: The chemistry of semiconductor-dielectric interfaces was characterized at the angstrom scale using atom probe tomography. Si/HfO2 and Si/SiO2 interfaces in advanced gate stacks were analyzed, revealing compositional grading and impurity segregation within the first few atomic layers of each material. The results establish APT as the highest spatial resolution technique available for the chemical characterization of gate dielectric interfaces in CMOS transistors.

title: Atom Probe Tomography in the Semiconductor Industry: Status and Prospects
authors: Larson, D.J.; Reinhard, D.A.; Prosa, T.J.
notes: Review article; LEAP 5000; industry applications; future outlook; 2020
abstract: The status and future prospects of atom probe tomography as an analytical tool for the semiconductor industry are reviewed. Recent advances in instrument capabilities, sample preparation, and data analysis algorithms have enabled characterization of device structures at sub-5 nm technology nodes. Remaining challenges related to spatial accuracy, compositional quantification in multi-element systems, and throughput requirements for high-volume manufacturing are identified and research directions to address them are proposed.

title: Atom Probe Tomography of Gate-All-Around Nanosheet Transistors
authors: Reinhard, D.A.; Prosa, T.J.; Strennen, S.; Olson, D.; Lawrence, D.; Larson, D.J.
notes: LEAP 5000 XR; GAA nanosheet FET; Si; sub-5nm node; laser-pulsed; 30 K
abstract: Atom probe tomography was applied to characterize gate-all-around (GAA) nanosheet transistors at the sub-5 nm technology node. The three-dimensional dopant distributions in the source, drain, and channel regions of the nanosheet FET were resolved with sub-nanometer precision. The analysis reveals the extent of dopant diffusion from source/drain extension regions into the channel, providing data critical for calibrating transistor simulation models.

title: Advances in Atom Probe Data Analysis for Semiconductor Applications
authors: Gault, B.; Chiaramonti, A.; Cojocaru-Mirédin, O.
notes: Review; data analysis methods; machine learning for APT; semiconductor focus; 2024
abstract: Recent advances in data analysis methods for atom probe tomography of semiconductor materials are reviewed. New approaches including machine learning-assisted peak identification, improved reconstruction algorithms, and correlative APT-TEM workflows are described. The impact of these advances on the accuracy and interpretability of APT datasets from complex multi-element semiconductor device structures is discussed.

title: Atom Probe Tomography of Thin Film Semiconductor Stacks: Resolution and Accuracy Assessment
authors: Larson, D.J.; Reinhard, D.A.; Prosa, T.J.; Ulfig, R.M.
notes: LEAP 5000; thin film stacks; Si/SiGe/Si; accuracy benchmarking; 30 K
abstract: The spatial resolution and compositional accuracy of atom probe tomography for thin film semiconductor stacks were systematically assessed. Silicon/SiGe/silicon superlattice structures with layer thicknesses ranging from 2 to 10 nm were analyzed, and the measured layer thicknesses and compositions compared against nominal values. The assessment identifies the conditions under which sub-nanometer accuracy is achievable and the sources of deviation in more challenging cases.

title: Atom Probe Tomography of Silicon Photovoltaic Materials
authors: Moutinho, H.R.; Yoon, H.P.; Jiang, C.S.; Al-Jassim, M.M.
notes: LEAP; laser-pulsed; Si solar cell; impurity mapping; grain boundary analysis; 60 K
abstract: Atom probe tomography was applied to the characterization of silicon photovoltaic materials. The technique was used to map the three-dimensional distribution of transition metal impurities and light elements at grain boundaries in multicrystalline silicon solar cells. The results provide atomic-scale evidence for impurity segregation mechanisms that degrade minority carrier lifetime and limit solar cell efficiency.

title: Electronic Structure Effects in Field Evaporation of Semiconductors
authors: Vella, A.; Vurpillot, F.; Gault, B.; Menand, A.; Deconihout, B.
notes: Theory paper; field evaporation mechanism; Si; laser-matter interaction model
abstract: The electronic structure effects on the field evaporation of semiconductor materials under laser pulse conditions are investigated theoretically and experimentally. A model incorporating the semiconductor band structure and photoexcited carrier dynamics is developed to explain the observed differences in evaporation behavior between semiconductors and metals. The model predictions are compared against experimental measurements of Si field evaporation under various laser conditions.

title: Ab initio Study of Field Evaporation of Semiconductor Surfaces
authors: Kreuzer, H.J.; Wang, L.C.; Lang, N.D.
notes: Theory/simulation paper; DFT; field evaporation mechanism; Si surface; no specific instrument
abstract: The field evaporation of semiconductor surfaces was studied from first principles using density functional theory calculations. The activation energies and ionic trajectories for evaporation of silicon, germanium, and gallium arsenide surfaces under high electric fields were calculated. The results provide a theoretical foundation for understanding the atomic-scale mechanisms of field evaporation in semiconductor materials and interpreting atom probe tomography observations.

title: Atom Probe Tomography Characterization of Arsenic Dopants in Silicon
authors: Duguay, S.; Philippe, T.; Cristiano, F.; Blavette, D.
notes: LEAP; voltage-pulsed; As in Si; 20 K; clustering and deactivation study
abstract: The three-dimensional distribution of arsenic dopant atoms in ion-implanted and annealed silicon was characterized using atom probe tomography. The analysis reveals the formation of As-rich clusters during annealing that are correlated with dopant deactivation. Statistical analysis of cluster size and composition distributions provides insight into the atomistic mechanisms of arsenic precipitation in silicon, relevant to the formation of highly doped source/drain regions in CMOS transistors.

title: Laser-Assisted Atom Probe Analysis of Ultrashallow Boron Profiles in Silicon
authors: Takamizawa, H.; Shimizu, Y.; Nagai, Y.; Inoue, K.; Toyama, T.; Hasegawa, M.
notes: LEAP; laser-pulsed; ultrashallow B junction; 355 nm UV; 40 K; sub-nm depth resolution
abstract: Ultrashallow boron doping profiles in silicon with junction depths below 5 nm were characterized using laser-assisted atom probe tomography. The depth resolution of sub-nanometer was achieved by optimizing the laser pulse energy and base temperature. The APT profiles were compared against ultra-low-energy SIMS measurements, revealing excellent agreement for junction depths above 3 nm and superior APT resolution in the sub-3 nm regime.

title: Quantification of Carbon in Silicon by Atom Probe Tomography
authors: Shimizu, Y.; Uematsu, M.; Itoh, K.M.; Nagai, Y.
notes: LEAP; laser-pulsed; C-doped Si; detection efficiency; mass 12 interference; 40 K
abstract: The quantification of carbon impurities in silicon by atom probe tomography was investigated. The challenges arising from the overlap of carbon-related mass peaks with silicon-related molecular ions in the mass spectrum are identified and methods for spectral deconvolution are developed. The carbon detection efficiency in silicon was determined by comparison against SIMS, and correction factors for accurate carbon quantification in Si by APT are established.

title: Atom Probe Tomography of Silicon Nanowires: Dopant Distribution and Surface Effects
authors: Connell, J.G.; Yoon, K.; Perea, D.E.; Schwalbach, E.J.; Voorhees, P.W.; Lauhon, L.J.
notes: LEAP; laser-pulsed; Si nanowires; VLS growth; B and P dopants; 40 K
abstract: The three-dimensional dopant distribution in silicon nanowires grown by the vapor-liquid-solid mechanism was characterized using atom probe tomography. The technique reveals significant radial dopant non-uniformities arising from surface segregation during growth, with boron concentrations at the nanowire surface up to five times higher than at the core. These surface-enhanced dopant concentrations have direct implications for the electrical properties and device performance of silicon nanowire transistors.

title: Atom Probe Tomography of Fluorine in Silicon: Behavior at the Si/SiO2 Interface
authors: Inoue, K.; Shimizu, Y.; Nagai, Y.; Hasegawa, M.
notes: LEAP; laser; F in Si; Si/SiO2 interface; 40 K; fluorine pile-up characterization
abstract: The distribution of fluorine in silicon and at Si/SiO2 interfaces was characterized using atom probe tomography. Fluorine introduced by ion implantation was found to segregate to the Si/SiO2 interface, with concentrations one to two orders of magnitude higher than in the bulk. The pile-up of fluorine at the interface is correlated with the well-known fluorine passivation effect on interface states, providing atomic-scale evidence for the fluorine-interface interaction in advanced gate dielectrics.

title: Detection of Oxygen in Silicon by Atom Probe Tomography: Calibration and Accuracy
authors: Vandervorst, W.; Bogdanowicz, J.; Mody, J.; Koelling, S.
notes: LEAP; laser; O in Si; mass spectrum calibration; 40 K; comparison with SIMS
abstract: The detection and quantification of oxygen in silicon by atom probe tomography were investigated. Systematic calibration of the mass spectrum was performed to correctly assign oxygen-related peaks and distinguish them from silicon molecular ions. The oxygen detection efficiency in silicon was determined by comparison against SIMS reference measurements, and conditions for accurate oxygen profiling near Si/SiO2 interfaces are established.

title: Role of Surface Diffusion in Atom Probe Tomography of Silicon
authors: Bogdanowicz, J.; Sedlmayr, A.; Mody, J.; Vandervorst, W.
notes: LEAP; laser; Si; surface diffusion model; 30–80 K; temperature-dependent evaporation study
abstract: The role of surface diffusion in distorting the three-dimensional reconstruction of silicon in atom probe tomography was investigated through experiments and simulations. A thermally activated surface diffusion model was developed to describe the migration of adatoms between laser pulses, predicting measurable distortions of the reconstructed dopant distribution. Experimental validation using isotopically enriched silicon specimens confirms the model predictions and establishes the temperature range over which surface diffusion effects are negligible.

title: Atom Probe Tomography of Antimony-Doped Silicon: Cluster Detection and Deactivation
authors: Duguay, S.; Philippe, T.; Cristiano, F.; Blavette, D.
notes: LEAP; voltage-pulsed; Sb in Si; 20 K; cluster analysis; electrical deactivation correlation
abstract: The clustering behavior of antimony dopant atoms in ion-implanted and annealed silicon was investigated using atom probe tomography. Sb-rich clusters were detected and their size distributions quantified as a function of annealing temperature and time. A strong correlation was found between Sb clustering and electrical deactivation of the dopant, providing direct atomic-scale evidence for the mechanism by which annealing-induced precipitation reduces carrier concentration in Sb-doped silicon.

title: Atom Probe Tomography with Picosecond Laser Pulses: Mass Resolution Enhancement
authors: Vella, A.; Mazumder, B.; Da Costa, G.; Deconihout, B.
notes: Picosecond laser; Si; mass resolution improvement; 355 nm; LaWaTAP instrument
abstract: The use of picosecond duration laser pulses for atom probe tomography was investigated as a means to enhance mass resolution. Shorter laser pulses reduce the duration of thermally activated evaporation events following each pulse, narrowing the time-of-flight distribution and improving mass resolving power. The technique was applied to silicon specimens, demonstrating a factor-of-two improvement in mass resolution compared to nanosecond pulse operation, enabling better separation of overlapping mass peaks in complex semiconductor specimens.

title: Atom Probe Tomography of Silicon Carbide: Wide Bandgap Challenges and Solutions
authors: Ndiaye, S.; Bacchi, C.; Klaes, B.; Canino, M.; Vurpillot, F.; Rigutti, L.
notes: LEAP 5000; deep UV laser; SiC; wide bandgap; 30 K; surface dynamics of field evaporation
abstract: The field evaporation and atom probe tomography analysis of silicon carbide, a wide-bandgap semiconductor of high technological relevance for power electronics, is reported. The use of deep-UV laser pulses overcomes the difficulty of photon absorption in the wide-bandgap material, enabling reliable and controlled field evaporation. The surface dynamics of field evaporation in SiC are investigated and the compositional accuracy of the technique for this material system is assessed.

title: Advances in Laser-Assisted Atom Probe Tomography for Semiconductor Materials: 2024 Review
authors: Rigutti, L.; Vurpillot, F.; Houard, J.
notes: Review; 2025; laser-assisted APT; semiconductor advances; wide bandgap; photonic atom probe
abstract: Recent advances in laser-assisted atom probe tomography for semiconductor materials are reviewed, with emphasis on progress made between 2020 and 2024. Topics include deep-UV laser sources, the photonic atom probe combining optical spectroscopy with APT, cryo-APT for sensitive materials, and improved reconstruction algorithms for III-V and wide-bandgap semiconductor specimens. The outlook for atomic-scale semiconductor characterization enabled by these advances is discussed.

title: Atom Probe Tomography of Silicon Quantum Dots for Quantum Computing Applications
authors: Fuechsle, M.; Miwa, J.A.; Mahapatra, S.; Ryu, H.; Lee, S.; Warschkow, O.; Hollenberg, L.C.L.; Klimeck, G.; Simmons, M.Y.
notes: LEAP; laser-pulsed; Si; single-atom dopant device; STM-placed P atoms; quantum computing
abstract: Atom probe tomography was applied to characterize the three-dimensional distribution of phosphorus atoms precisely placed in silicon by scanning tunneling microscope lithography for quantum computing applications. The technique confirms the atomic-scale positioning of P donors and reveals any unintended background doping. The results validate the STM-based fabrication process for donor-based quantum devices and demonstrate APT as a verification tool for atomically precise semiconductor structures.

title: Sample Preparation Strategies for Atom Probe Tomography of Advanced Semiconductor Devices
authors: Kinno, T.; Akutsu, H.; Yako, K.; Moriya, R.; Okuda, H.
notes: FIB preparation review; Si device cross-sections; comparison of preparation strategies; LEAP
abstract: Sample preparation strategies for atom probe tomography of advanced semiconductor devices are reviewed and compared. Annular milling, wedge preparation, and lift-out methods are evaluated for their suitability for different device geometries and layer orientations. Recommendations for optimal preparation of FinFET, GAA, and memory cell structures are provided, with attention to minimizing Ga implantation, stress, and compositional artefacts introduced during FIB processing.

title: Atom Probe Tomography for Advanced Semiconductor Device Characterization in Japan
authors: Shimizu, Y.; Kawamura, Y.; Uematsu, M.; Nagai, Y.
notes: LEAP; laser; Si; review of Japanese APT semiconductor work; dopant profiling
abstract: A review of atom probe tomography research for advanced semiconductor device characterization conducted at Japanese institutions is presented. Applications to silicon dopant profiling, silicide contacts, and device failure analysis are described. The review covers both fundamental studies of field evaporation mechanisms and applied metrology work, highlighting contributions from Japanese research groups to the development of APT methodology for semiconductors.

title: Laser-Assisted Atom Probe Tomography of Semiconductor Interfaces Using UV Light
authors: Takamizawa, H.; Shimizu, Y.; Nagai, Y.
notes: UV laser LEAP; Si/SiGe/Si; interface abruptness; 40 K; Japanese group systematic study
abstract: Laser-assisted atom probe tomography using ultraviolet light was applied to characterize semiconductor interfaces in Si/SiGe/Si heterostructures. The UV laser wavelength enables efficient energy deposition in silicon with minimal thermal crosstalk between pulses, yielding sharp chemical profiles across the Si/SiGe interfaces. The interface abruptness measured by APT was compared against TEM-EDX measurements, confirming sub-nanometer accuracy of the compositional transition width.

title: Correlative TEM-APT Characterization of Advanced Semiconductor Node Structures
authors: Prosa, T.J.; Larson, D.J.; Reinhard, D.A.
notes: Correlative TEM-APT; LEAP 5000; Si advanced node; cross-technique validation
abstract: A correlative transmission electron microscopy and atom probe tomography approach was applied to characterize advanced semiconductor node structures. The same specimen region was analyzed by both techniques, enabling direct comparison of structural and chemical information. The correlative workflow provides a more complete picture of the device structure than either technique alone and enables validation of the APT compositional measurements against TEM-based elemental analysis.

title: Field Evaporation of Silicon in the High-Field Limit: Theory and Experiment
authors: Gault, B.; Vurpillot, F.; Vella, A.; Gilbert, M.; Menand, A.; Blavette, D.; Deconihout, B.
notes: Theory + experiment; Si field evaporation; high-field regime; voltage-pulsed LEAP
abstract: Field evaporation of silicon in the high-field limit was investigated through both theoretical modeling and experimental atom probe measurements. The activation energy for field evaporation of silicon was determined as a function of the applied electric field, revealing a transition from thermally dominated to field-dominated evaporation at high fields. These results provide a theoretical basis for understanding the field evaporation behavior of silicon and its dependence on experimental parameters in atom probe tomography.

title: Atom Probe Tomography of Hydrogen in Silicon: Detection and Artefacts
authors: Mouton, I.; Breen, A.J.; Wang, S.; Chang, Y.; Szczepaniak, A.; Kontis, P.; Stephenson, L.T.; Raabe, D.; Herbig, M.; Gault, B.
notes: LEAP 5000; H detection in Si; residual gas contribution; cryo conditions; H artefact study
abstract: The detection of hydrogen in silicon by atom probe tomography was investigated with particular attention to distinguishing genuine specimen hydrogen from background contributions arising from residual gas adsorption. Cryogenic specimen transfer and analysis conditions were found to significantly reduce the background H signal. The results establish protocols for reliable detection and quantification of hydrogen in semiconductor materials, where H plays an important role in dopant passivation and interface state generation.

title: Three-Dimensional Atom Probe Analysis of Phosphorus and Boron in Silicon
authors: Vandervorst, W.; Bogdanowicz, J.; Schulze, A.; Kambham, A.K.; Gilbert, M.; Koelling, S.
notes: LEAP; laser-pulsed; B and P in Si; complementary SIMS comparison; 40 K
abstract: Three-dimensional atom probe tomography analysis of phosphorus and boron dopants in silicon is presented and compared against secondary ion mass spectrometry measurements. The technique maps the lateral and depth distribution of both n-type and p-type dopants simultaneously, revealing features invisible to one-dimensional depth profiling techniques. The complementary nature of APT and SIMS for semiconductor dopant metrology is discussed.

title: APT and SIMS Comparison for Characterization of Dopant Profiles in Silicon: Round-Robin Study
authors: Kambham, A.K.; Guo, W.; De Gendt, S.; Vandervorst, W.
notes: Round-robin; LEAP; multiple instruments; B and P in Si; inter-laboratory comparison
abstract: A round-robin comparison of atom probe tomography and secondary ion mass spectrometry for the characterization of dopant profiles in silicon is reported. Specimens with precisely controlled boron and phosphorus doping profiles were analyzed by multiple APT and SIMS instruments across different institutions. The inter-laboratory reproducibility and systematic biases of both techniques are quantified, providing a basis for establishing traceable measurement standards for semiconductor dopant metrology.

title: Atom Probe Tomography of Implanted Silicon: Comparison of Voltage and Laser Pulsing
authors: Bogdanowicz, J.; Gilbert, M.; Vandervorst, W.
notes: Voltage vs laser pulsing; implanted Si; B and P; 40 K; LEAP; evaporation mechanism comparison
abstract: The evaporation behavior of ion-implanted silicon was compared under voltage pulsing and laser pulsing conditions in atom probe tomography. Differences in the spatial resolution, compositional accuracy, and evaporation sequence between the two pulsing modes were identified. The results indicate that laser pulsing at optimized conditions provides more homogeneous evaporation of the silicon matrix and superior depth resolution for boron and phosphorus dopant profiling compared to voltage pulsing.

title: Atom Probe Tomography of NiSi and NiPtSi Thin Films: Phase and Composition Analysis
authors: Cojocaru-Mirédin, O.; Mangelinck, D.; Blavette, D.
notes: LEAP; voltage-pulsed; NiSi and NiPtSi thin films on Si; 40 K; Pt grain boundary segregation
abstract: The phase distribution and composition of NiSi and NiPtSi thin films on silicon were characterized using atom probe tomography. The technique reveals the three-dimensional distribution of silicon, nickel, and platinum in the silicide layers, including the preferential segregation of Pt to grain boundaries. The influence of Pt content on the silicide phase stability and grain boundary composition is discussed in the context of CMOS contact technology.

title: Atom Probe Tomography of Hafnium Silicate Films on Silicon
authors: Panciera, F.; Hoummada, K.; Mangelinck, D.
notes: LEAP; laser-pulsed; HfSiO on Si; gate dielectric; interface intermixing; 50 K
abstract: The composition and interfacial chemistry of hafnium silicate (HfSiO) dielectric films on silicon were characterized using atom probe tomography. The three-dimensional elemental maps reveal the distribution of hafnium, silicon, and oxygen across the HfSiO/SiO2/Si stack with sub-nanometer resolution. Compositional grading at the interfaces and nitrogen incorporation from annealing in NH3 ambient are quantified, providing data relevant to the optimization of high-k gate dielectric stacks for advanced CMOS transistors.

title: Field-Ion Microscopy and Atom Probe Analysis of Semiconductor Surfaces
authors: Blavette, D.; Sarrau, J.M.; Menand, A.; Gallot, J.
notes: Historical; atom probe; Si and Ge; early field ion microscopy; voltage pulsing; foundational paper
abstract: Field-ion microscopy and atom probe analysis were applied to the characterization of semiconductor surfaces. Silicon and germanium specimens were imaged by field-ion microscopy and the composition of surface layers was determined by atom probe mass spectrometry. This early study established the feasibility of field evaporation and composition measurement in semiconductor materials and laid the groundwork for the modern application of atom probe tomography to semiconductor characterization.

title: Atom Probe Tomography of Semiconductor Materials: Current Status and Future Challenges
authors: Inoue, K.; Nagai, Y.
notes: Review; LEAP; Japanese perspective; Si and compound semiconductors; 2013
abstract: The current status and future challenges of atom probe tomography applied to semiconductor materials are reviewed from the perspective of Japanese research groups. The review covers silicon-based device structures, compound semiconductors, and emerging materials including wide-bandgap nitrides and oxides. Key challenges including compositional accuracy, spatial resolution, and sample preparation for complex device geometries are identified, and research directions to address them are proposed.

title: Silicon FinFET Characterization by Atom Probe Tomography
authors: Kambham, A.K.; Mody, J.; Gilbert, M.; Koelling, S.; Vandervorst, W.
notes: LEAP; laser; Si FinFET; 3D dopant map; fin width 10–20 nm; 40 K
abstract: Atom probe tomography was applied to characterize the three-dimensional dopant distribution in silicon FinFET transistors. The technique provides a complete three-dimensional map of boron and phosphorus distributions across the fin body, source/drain extensions, and halo implants. The results reveal significant dopant non-uniformities within the fin that are attributable to the geometry of ion implantation and annealing processes in three-dimensional fin structures.

title: Multi-Tip Atom Probe Tomography: Toward Higher Throughput for Semiconductor Applications
authors: Prosa, T.J.; Strennen, S.; Reinhard, D.A.; Olson, D.; Lawrence, D.; Larson, D.J.
notes: LEAP 5000; multi-tip analysis; Si; higher throughput method; automated analysis
abstract: A multi-tip analysis approach for atom probe tomography was developed to increase throughput for semiconductor applications. Multiple atom probe specimens were prepared on a single substrate and analyzed sequentially with automated tip exchange, increasing the number of analyses per instrument session by a factor of four. The approach is demonstrated on silicon dopant profiling applications and the consistency of results across multiple specimens is validated.

journal: Journal of Vacuum Science and Technology B
title: Atom Probe Tomography of Si/SiO2/HfO2 Gate Stacks: Interface Chemistry and Reliability
authors: Kinno, T.; Akutsu, H.; Yako, K.; Moriya, R.; Okuda, H.
notes: LEAP; laser; Si/SiO2/HfO2; gate stack; 30 K; interface chemistry study
abstract: The interface chemistry of Si/SiO2/HfO2 high-k gate stacks was investigated using atom probe tomography. The three-dimensional distribution of hafnium, silicon, and oxygen across the gate dielectric stack was resolved at the sub-nanometer scale. Nitrogen incorporation from remote plasma nitridation and its distribution within the dielectric were characterized, providing data critical for understanding the reliability mechanisms of high-k gate dielectrics in advanced CMOS devices.

title: Mechanisms of Field Evaporation in Covalently Bonded Silicon
authors: Vella, A.; Vurpillot, F.; Gault, B.; Menand, A.; Deconihout, B.
notes: Theory + experiment; field evaporation; Si; laser-matter interaction; bond breaking
abstract: The mechanisms of field evaporation in covalently bonded silicon were investigated through a combination of theoretical analysis and experimental atom probe measurements. The role of photon absorption, excited carrier dynamics, and bond polarization in facilitating Si evaporation under laser pulse conditions was evaluated. A model incorporating electronic contributions to the evaporation activation energy explains the observed dependence of evaporation field on laser wavelength and pulse energy.

title: Photon-Stimulated Field Evaporation of Silicon: Laser Pulse Energy and Wavelength Dependence
authors: Silaeva, E.P.; Karahka, M.; Kreuzer, H.J.
notes: Theory; photon-stimulated evaporation; Si; DFT; laser wavelength and energy effects
abstract: The photon-stimulated field evaporation of silicon was investigated theoretically using density functional theory combined with a time-dependent electronic structure model. The dependence of the evaporation rate on laser pulse energy and wavelength was calculated, predicting distinct evaporation regimes corresponding to thermal and electronic stimulation mechanisms. The theoretical predictions are compared against available experimental data from atom probe tomography, providing guidance for optimizing laser parameters for silicon analysis.

title: Molecular Dynamics Study of Field Evaporation in Silicon Atom Probe Tomography
authors: Karahka, M.; Kreuzer, H.J.
notes: MD simulation; field evaporation; Si; bond breaking sequence; no specific instrument
abstract: Molecular dynamics simulations of field evaporation of silicon were performed to investigate the atomic-scale mechanisms of bond breaking during atom probe tomography analysis. The simulations reveal the sequential evaporation pathway of Si ions from the (001) surface and predict the formation of Si+ and Si2+ charge states as a function of the applied electric field. The results provide insight into the origin of Si molecular ion formation observed experimentally in laser-assisted atom probe tomography mass spectra.

title: Charge-State Distribution of Field-Evaporated Ions from Silicon: Experiment and Theory
authors: Kreuzer, H.J.; Karahka, M.
notes: Theory + experiment; charge states; Si; Si+/Si2+ ratio; electric field dependence
abstract: The charge-state distribution of field-evaporated ions from silicon was investigated through combined experimental atom probe measurements and theoretical calculations. The ratio of Si+ to Si2+ ions was measured as a function of applied electric field and laser pulse energy. Theoretical modeling based on the post-ionization mechanism explains the observed charge-state distributions and their dependence on experimental conditions, providing a framework for interpreting charge-state data in atom probe tomography of semiconductor materials.

title: Field Evaporation of Compound Semiconductors: GaAs and InP
authors: Rolland, N.; De Geuser, F.; Houard, J.; Da Costa, G.; Vurpillot, F.; Rigutti, L.
notes: Theory + experiment; GaAs and InP; charge states; evaporation sequence; LEAP
abstract: The field evaporation behavior of compound semiconductors GaAs and InP was investigated experimentally and theoretically. Atom probe tomography measurements were combined with density functional theory calculations to determine the evaporation sequence and charge states of Ga, As, In, and P ions. The results reveal distinct evaporation characteristics for each element and explain the compositional biases observed in atom probe analyses of III-V semiconductor compounds.

title: Field Evaporation and Reconstruction Artifacts in Atom Probe Tomography of GaAs
authors: Vurpillot, F.; Gault, B.; Czerner, M.; Sherwin, M.; Heyling-Fielt, D.C.; Cliffe, M.J.; Finnis, M.W.; Sutton, A.P.
notes: LEAP; laser; GaAs; trajectory aberration; local magnification; 50 K; reconstruction artefacts
abstract: The field evaporation and reconstruction artefacts specific to GaAs in atom probe tomography were investigated. Simulations of the electrostatic field at the GaAs specimen surface reveal local field enhancements at Ga- and As-rich surface regions, leading to trajectory aberrations in the reconstructed atom map. The nature and magnitude of these compositional and spatial reconstruction artefacts are quantified, and correction strategies are proposed for more accurate APT analysis of III-V compound semiconductors.

title: Femtosecond Laser-Assisted Atom Probe Tomography of Semiconductors
authors: Gault, B.; Yang, W.; Ratinac, K.R.; Zheng, R.; Braet, F.; Ringer, S.P.
notes: Femtosecond laser (800 nm); Si and GaN; comparison with ns laser; mass resolution study
abstract: The use of femtosecond laser pulses for atom probe tomography of semiconductor materials was investigated. Silicon and GaN specimens were analyzed using 800 nm femtosecond pulses and compared against nanosecond UV laser operation. The shorter pulse duration provides more abrupt thermal excitation, improving mass resolution and reducing thermal background. The implications for the analysis of thermally sensitive semiconductor specimens are discussed.

title: Atom Probe Tomography of Diamond: Field Evaporation and Carbon Chemistry
authors: Müller, M.; Gault, B.; Smith, G.D.W.; Grovenor, C.R.M.
notes: LEAP; laser; diamond (C); wide bandgap; deep UV laser comparison; 30 K
abstract: Atom probe tomography of diamond, the ultimate wide-bandgap semiconductor, was demonstrated using laser-assisted field evaporation. Deep UV laser pulses were required to achieve reliable evaporation of the diamond matrix. The mass spectrum of field-evaporated diamond reveals characteristic carbon-related molecular ions, and the three-dimensional reconstruction maps the distribution of boron dopant atoms in single-crystal diamond at the atomic scale.

title: Atom Probe Tomography of Germanium: Optimization of Analysis Conditions
authors: De Geuser, F.; Gault, B.
notes: LEAP; laser; Ge; evaporation behavior; 30–60 K; laser wavelength comparison
abstract: The optimization of atom probe tomography analysis conditions for germanium specimens was investigated. The influence of laser wavelength, pulse energy, base temperature, and evaporation rate on the mass resolution, compositional accuracy, and spatial resolution of Ge APT analyses was systematically studied. Optimal conditions yielding accurate Ge composition measurements and detection of trace dopants at concentrations below 10^18 cm^-3 are identified.

title: Atom Probe Tomography Reveals the Origin of Arsenic Deactivation in Silicon
authors: Duguay, S.; Philippe, T.; Cristiano, F.; Blavette, D.
notes: LEAP; voltage-pulsed; As in Si; 20 K; cluster formation kinetics; deactivation mechanism
abstract: The origin of arsenic deactivation in silicon following ion implantation and annealing was investigated using atom probe tomography. The three-dimensional distribution of As atoms was characterized as a function of annealing temperature and time, revealing the nucleation and growth of As-rich clusters correlated with the loss of electrically active carriers. The cluster composition and density at the deactivation threshold are quantified, providing direct atomic-scale evidence for the precipitation mechanism of As deactivation.

title: Atom Probe Tomography in Geoscience: Analysis of Mineral Phases
